title: Boosting: Learning from Mistakes
concept: Boosting is an ensemble strategy that trains base models sequentially, where each new model is designed to correct the prediction errors of the models that came before it.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between bagging and standard single trees?

options.text: Bagging trains many trees in parallel on random bootstrap samples to reduce variance.
Bagging builds one deep tree and prunes it.
Bagging does not use decision trees.

exercises.explain: Correct! Bagging trains multiple trees in parallel to stabilize predictions.

cards.title: Learning from a graded quiz
cards.body: Imagine taking a 10-question practice test. You get questions 3 and 7 wrong. When you study for the next quiz, do you spend equal time on all 10 questions? No. You focus almost entirely on chapters 3 and 7 to correct your mistakes. That sequential improvement loop is the core of boosting.

cards.title: Boosting: Sequential training
cards.body: Unlike bagging (where models train in parallel independently), boosting trains models **sequentially**. Model 1 runs first. The algorithm measures its errors, then trains Model 2 specifically to correct those mistakes. Model 3 then corrects the combined mistakes of 1 and 2.

cards.title: Passing the baton in a relay
cards.body: Think of boosting like a relay team writing a report. Author 1 writes the draft. Author 2 reads it, spots the typos and missing details, and writes a revision focusing only on those errors. Author 3 checks the revision, fixes whatever is still wrong, and adds final polish. Each author builds directly on the work of the last.

cards.title: Targeting hard emails
cards.body: An email classifier (Model 1) gets 80 out of 100 emails correct, but consistently mistakes spam emails containing 'crypto' as normal. In the next step, the boosting algorithm forces Model 2 to focus on 'crypto' spam. Model 2 corrects these errors. The final prediction combines both models.

cards.title: Boosting is not faster than bagging
cards.body: Because bagging models train in parallel, you can split them across different processor cores. In boosting, because Model 2 *must* wait for Model 1's errors, you cannot train them in parallel. Boosting takes longer to train, but often yields higher final accuracy.

left.text:
Models train independently at the same time.
Each looks at random rows.
Reduces variance.

right.text:
Models train one after another.
Each model corrects the errors of the last.
Reduces bias.

exercises.prompt: What is the key characteristic of the boosting training process?

options.text: Models are trained sequentially, each focusing on the errors of the previous ones.
Models are trained in parallel with zero communication between them.
Features are randomly deleted to speed up training.
It uses unsupervised clustering to group error rows.

exercises.explain: Correct! Boosting is a sequential ensemble method where models learn from previous models' mistakes.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Because boosting is sequential, it can be easily parallelized across multiple server cores to speed up training times.
exercises.explain: False! Since Model N requires the errors of Model N-1, boosting models must train in order, making parallelization difficult.

exercises.sentence: While bagging focuses on reducing variance, boosting focuses on reducing ___.
exercises.explain: Boosting reduces bias by sequentially building models that correct systematic training errors.

options.text: Model 1 is trained -> Model 1's errors are highlighted -> Model 2 is trained to predict those highlighted errors.
Three models are trained on three separate bootstrap samples at the same time.
A decision tree is pruned to a max depth of 3.

exercises.explain: Correct! Sequential training with error highlighting is the core of boosting.

exercises.prompt: You train a boosting model with 10 sequential trees. Tree 1 makes errors on 20% of the data. How will Tree 2 be trained?

options.text: The algorithm highlights those 20% error rows, forcing Tree 2 to focus its questions on correcting them.
Tree 2 is trained on the 80% correct rows to keep it accurate.
Tree 2 is trained on a completely random dataset.
Tree 2 is trained in parallel with Tree 1.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The boosting algorithm highlights the mistake rows, steering Tree 2's training toward resolving those specific errors. Next, let's look at the building blocks of these sequential models!
